report_title: Youth Development and Economic Transformation in Timor-Leste

key_results.finding: About 10% of youth aged 15–34 experience well-being deprivation, including 11% of men and 9% of women.
key_results.significance: The result identifies a measurable gender difference in youth well-being deprivation.

key_results.finding: At the time of the survey, 82% of youth did not have jobs; separately, 46% were studying or undergoing training.
key_results.significance: The two reported measures show limited employment alongside substantial participation in education or training.

key_results.finding: The private sector provides employment to only 5% of the workforce.
key_results.significance: The small private-sector employment share highlights limited formal employment opportunities.

key_results.finding: The Government should consider allocating 25% of its budget to education and training.
key_results.significance: The recommendation links public investment to improved access to quality education and training.

key_results.finding: Timor-Leste has taken steps towards its goal of upper-middle-income status, while youth employment and skills remain major challenges.
key_results.significance: Progress towards the national income goal depends on expanding youth skills and productive employment.
